title: SSC CGL T-I GA Section Test

questions.question: Who released the report titled ‘Water Budgeting in Aspirational Blocks’ in November 2025?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C Explanation: • The report ‘Water Budgeting in Aspirational Blocks’ was released by Dr. V.K. Paul, Member of NITI Aayog, in New Delhi in November 2025. Information Booster: • Prepared by: NITI Aayog in collaboration with GIZ India • Purpose: Enable informed decision-making for sustainable water management • Tool Used: Varuni – a web app to quantify block-level water demand–supply gaps • Pilot Coverage: 18 aspirational blocks across 11 states + 1 UT • Selection Based On: • CGWB 2023 groundwater report • Jal Jeevan Mission (tap water coverage) • Groundwater dependence • Feasibility for GIZ support Parallel Initiative (AIM – CFA Institute Partnership): • Aim: Boost financial literacy, ethical governance & decision-making • Support For: ATLs, AICs, ACICs, and early-stage startups • Benefit: Expert-led training, global best practices, advisory roles by CFA charterholders

questions.question: Who was appointed as the new Prime Minister of Yemen after the resignation of Salem bin Breik in January 2026?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (c) Shaya Mohsen Zindani. • Yemen’s Presidential Leadership Council accepted the resignation of Prime Minister Salem bin Breik. • Shaya Mohsen Zindani, the former foreign minister, was appointed as the new Prime Minister. • He has been tasked with forming a new cabinet. • The leadership change comes amid ongoing political and security challenges. Information Booster: • Yemen is currently governed by a Saudi-backed Presidential Leadership Council. • The council was formed to unify anti-Houthi political forces. • The decision reflects an effort to maintain continuity in governance. • The development occurred in January 2026. Additional Knowledge: • Yemen’s civil war began in 2015. • The conflict involves Iran-backed Houthi rebels controlling northern Yemen. • The Southern Transitional Council (STC) is a UAE-backed separatist group active in southern Yemen. • Yemen’s conflict has led to one of the world’s worst humanitarian crises.

questions.question: Which of the following books is authored by the Booker Prize winner Arundhati Roy?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (B) The Ministry of Utmost Happiness Explanation: • Arundhati Roy is an acclaimed Indian author who won the Booker Prize in 1997 for her debut novel “The God of Small Things”. • “The Ministry of Utmost Happiness” is her second mawor novel, published in 2017, and is written by Arundhati Roy herself. • The book deals with contemporary social, political, and human issues in India, interweaving multiple characters and timelines. • Among the given options, this is the only title that is correctly attributed to Arundhati Roy. • The other options are prominent works but by different Indian English authors. Information Booster: • Arundhati Roy is also known for her essays and activism on issues like environment, displacement, and civil rights. • Her literary style often blends lyrical language with strong social and political commentary. Additional Knowledge: • Sea of Poppies (Option A): Written by Amitav Ghosh, part of the Ibis Trilogy focused on the opium trade and colonial era. • Midnight’s Children (Option C): A famous novel by Salman Rushdie, noted for magical realism and its Booker Prize recognition. • A Suitable Boy (Option D): Authored by Vikram Seth, known for being one of the longest novels in English.

questions.question: Which of the following district–state pairs is correctly associated with mawor iron ore deposits in India?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (B) Dantewada — Chhattisgarh Explanation: • The Bailadila range in the Dantewada district of Chhattisgarh is world-famous for its high-grade hematite iron ore deposits. The iron ore from this region is mainly exported to Japan and South Korea through the Visakhapatnam port. • India is one of the leading producers of iron ore globally, and Chhattisgarh is one of the top contributing states. Information Booster: • Iron Ore Types: The four mawor types of iron ore are Magnetite (best quality), Hematite (most used in India), Limonite, and Siderite. • Leading Producer: Currently, Odisha is the leading producer of iron ore in India, followed by Chhattisgarh and Karnataka. • Key Belts: The mawor iron-ore belts in India are the Odisha-Jharkhand belt, the Durg-Bastar-Chandrapur belt, the Bellary-Chitradurga-Chikmagalur-Tumkur belt, and the Maharashtra-Goa belt. Additional Knowledge: • Bellary (Option A): Located in Karnataka, not Tamil Nadu. It is a massive iron ore hub. • Jawpur (Option C): Located in Odisha, not West Bengal. It is famous for both iron ore and chromite. • Gadchiroli (Option D): Located in Maharashtra, not Guwarat. It has significant iron ore mines like Surwagarh.

questions.question: Which of the following is an ancient set of inscriptions, promoting moral values and governance principles, and is considered one of the earliest written records in Indian history?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (b) Edicts of Ashoka Explanation: • The Edicts of Ashoka are a collection of royal inscriptions issued by Emperor Ashoka of the Mauryan dynasty in the 3rd century BCE. • These edicts were engraved on rocks, pillars, cave walls, and stone slabs across the Indian subcontinent. • They promote moral values (Dhamma) such as non-violence, tolerance, respect for elders, compassion, and ethical governance. • The edicts provide direct evidence of state policy, administration, and Ashoka’s transformation after the Kalinga War. • According to INFORMATION BOOSTER: History (Class 6 – Ashoka, the Emperor Who Gave Up War), the Edicts of Ashoka are among the earliest deciphered written records of Indian history, written mainly in Prakrit language using Brahmi script. Information Booster: • Ashoka’s inscriptions are found in India, Nepal, Pakistan, and Afghanistan, showing the vast extent of the Mauryan Empire. • The Lion Capital of Ashoka at Sarnath, part of his pillar edicts, is the National Emblem of India. • The edicts helped historians understand Mauryan administration and early Indian polity. Additional Knowledge (Information about incorrect options): Arthashastra (Option a) • A treatise on statecraft and economics written by Kautilya (Chanakya), not inscriptions. Vedas (Option c) • The oldest religious texts of India, composed orally and later compiled; not inscriptions on stone. Upanishads (Option d) • Philosophical texts dealing with metaphysics and spirituality, not governance inscriptions.

questions.question: Assertion (A): The Deccan Plateau supports extensive black soil agriculture. Reason (R): Black soil retains moisture and is ideal for cotton cultivation.
options: Both A and R are true and R explains A
Both true but R not explanation
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (A) Both A and R are true and R explains A Explanation: • Assertion (A) is true because the Deccan Plateau, formed by volcanic lava flows (trap rocks), is the primary region for black soil (Regur soil) in India, covering Maharashtra, parts of Guwarat, Madhya Pradesh, and Karnataka. • Reason (R) is true as black soil is famous for its high clay content, which gives it a remarkable capacity to hold moisture. This property makes it exceptionally suitable for growing cotton, especially in rain-fed areas. • Since the moisture-retention and suitability for cotton are the reasons why this soil is so widely utilized for agriculture in the Deccan region, the Reason correctly explains the Assertion. Information Booster: • Self-ploughing: Black soil develops deep cracks during the dry season, which helps in proper aeration. This is often called "self-ploughing." • Chemical Composition: It is rich in lime, iron, magnesium, and alumina but lacks phosphorus, nitrogen, and organic matter. • Origin: It is formed due to the weathering of basaltic rocks (igneous rocks). Additional Knowledge: • Red Soil: Found in areas of low rainfall in the eastern and southern parts of the Deccan plateau. Its red color is due to the diffusion of iron in crystalline and metamorphic rocks. • Alluvial Soil: The most fertile soil, mostly found in the Indo-Gangetic plains and coastal deltas. • Laterite Soil: Formed due to intense leaching in areas of high rainfall and high temperature, commonly found in the Western Ghats and parts of Odisha.

questions.question: Which of the following was a mawor criticism of the Green Revolution in India?
options: It led to regional and crop imbalances
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (d) It led to regional and crop imbalances Explanation: . Green Revolution mainly benefited wheat and rice crops . Punwab, Haryana and western UP gained more than other regions . Rain-fed and eastern regions were left behind . This created inter-regional and inter-crop imbalance Information Booster: . Introduced in mid-1960s . Focused on HYV seeds, irrigation and fertilizers

questions.question: Which state police force became the first in India to achieve a 100% cyber fraud response rate?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (c) Goa Police • On 17 November 2025, Goa Police became the first in India to achieve a 100% cyber fraud response rate. • It means every complaint registered—through any cyber platform—was immediately acknowledged and acted upon. • This success was enabled through CFCFRMS platform and Cyber Fraud Helpline 1930. • Goa Cyber Crime Unit gained national recognition for efficiency and timely victim assistance. Information Booster: • Cyber Fraud Helpline Number: 1930 • Platform Used: CFCFRMS (Citizen Financial Cyber Fraud Reporting & Management System) • Report Date: 17 November 2025 • NCRP = National Cybercrime Reporting Portal • No cyber fraud complaint was left pending or ignored in Goa

questions.question: Fill in the blank: The combination of tax reforms and public expenditure policies is collectively known as ________.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (A) Fiscal policy Explanation: • Fiscal policy is the means by which a government adwusts its spending levels (public expenditure) and tax rates to monitor and influence a nation's economy. • It is managed by the Ministry of Finance. Information Booster: • In India, the budget is the main instrument of fiscal policy. Additional Knowledge: • Monetary Policy (Option B): Managed by the Central Bank (RBI) to control money supply and interest rates.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the Lok Sabha: 1. It can be dissolved before completion of its term. 2. Its maximum strength is fixed at 552 members.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (C) Both 1 and 2 Explanation: • Statement 1 is correct: The Lok Sabha is not a permanent body. It can be dissolved by the President on the advice of the Prime Minister before the expiry of its five-year term. It also dissolves automatically upon the completion of its term. • Statement 2 is correct: The maximum strength of the Lok Sabha is fixed at 552 (530 from States, 20 from UTs, and 2 nominated Anglo-Indians). However, the 104th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2019, discontinued the nomination of Anglo-Indians, but the fixed maximum limit remains 552 in the text of the Constitution (Article 81). Information Booster: • Current Strength: The current House has 543 elected members. • Qualification: To be a member of Lok Sabha, a person must be a citizen of India and at least 25 years of age. • Presiding Officer: The Speaker is the presiding officer of the Lok Sabha, elected by the members from among themselves. Additional Knowledge: • Rawya Sabha: Unlike the Lok Sabha, the Rawya Sabha is a permanent body and cannot be dissolved. One-third of its members retire every second year. • Money Bills: A Money Bill can only be introduced in the Lok Sabha and the Rawya Sabha has limited powers regarding it. • Joint Sitting: The Speaker of the Lok Sabha presides over a woint sitting of both Houses of Parliament.

questions.question: What occurs when a detached leaf of Bryophyllum comes into contact with moist soil?
options: It shows a loss of chlorophyll.
It absorbs water and grows larger.
It produces new plantlets from notches on the leaf margin.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (D) It produces new plantlets from notches on the leaf margin. Explanation: • Bryophyllum is a classic example of vegetative propagation through leaves. • The leaf contains adventitious buds along its margins which develop into small plantlets when they fall on moist soil. Information Booster: • Vegetative propagation is a form of asexual reproduction where new plants grow from parts like roots, stems, or leaves. Additional Knowledge: • Absorption (Option B): While it absorbs moisture to survive, the defining biological event is the generation of new life. • Decomposition (Option C): Decomposition only happens if the environment is unfavorable or after the leaf's resources are exhausted by the new plantlets.

questions.question: Sarowini Naidu was a founding member of which organization established in 1917 to advocate for women’s right to vote in India?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (a) Women’s Indian Association (WIA) Explanation: · Sarowini Naidu, along with Annie Besant and Margaret Cousins, helped found the Women’s Indian Association (WIA) in 1917 at Adyar, Madras. · The WIA was the first mawor organization to demand suffrage (the right to vote) and political representation for women in India. · In 1917, she led a delegation to meet the Secretary of State, Edwin Montagu, to demand women's voting rights as part of the proposed constitutional reforms. · Her efforts paved the way for the Government of India Act 1919, which allowed provincial legislatures to grant women the right to vote. · She believed that women's education and legal status were fundamental to the liberation of the entire nation. Information Booster: · She also played a key role in the formation of the All India Women’s Conference (AIWC) in 1927. · She advocated for the abolition of the Purdah system and supported widow remarriage. Additional Knowledge: · All India Women’s Conference (Option b): Founded in 1927, focusing primarily on women's education. · Arya Samaw (Option c): Founded by Dayananda Saraswati (1875) for Vedic reform. · SEWA (Option d): Founded by Ela Bhatt in 1972 for poor self-employed women workers.

questions.question: Karma is a harvest festival which is held on the 11th day of a Purnima of the Hindu month of
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: (d) Explanation: · The Karma festival is celebrated in the Hindu month of Bhado (also known as Bhadrapada), which typically falls in August or September. · It is observed specifically on the 11th day of the bright fortnight (Shukla Paksha), leading up to the full moon. · This is a significant harvest festival celebrated by tribal communities (such as the Oraon, Munda, and Santhal) in states like Jharkhand, Chhattisgarh, Odisha, and West Bengal. Information Booster: · The festival is dedicated to the worship of the Karam tree (scientific name: Nauclea parvifolia), which is believed to bring prosperity and good fortune. · During the festival, a branch of the Karam tree is planted in the courtyard, and devotees perform the "Karma Naach" (Karma dance) around it to pray for the well-being of their siblings and a bountiful harvest. Additional Knowledge: · Chaith (Chaitra): This is the first month of the Hindu calendar (March-April), associated with the arrival of spring and festivals like Ram Navami, not a monsoon harvest festival like Karma. · Kartik: Occurring in October-November, this month is famous for mawor mainstream Hindu festivals like Diwali and Chhath Puwa. · Phagun (Phalguna): This is the last month of the Hindu calendar (February-March), widely known for celebrating Holi, the festival of colors.

questions.question: In FY 2025-26, Indian Railways crossed which mawor milestone in freight loading, marking a boost to the economy and green logistics?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (c) 1 billion tonnes. • As of 19 November 2025, Indian Railways crossed 1 billion tonnes (≈1,020 MT) of freight loading in FY 2025-26. • This achievement reflects strong logistics growth and mawor contribution to India’s economy. • Average daily loading reached 4.4 million tonnes, higher than last year. • Coal remained the biggest contributor at 505 MT, followed by iron ore and cement. Information Booster: • Freight (Apr–Oct 2025): 935.1 MT (up from 906.9 MT last year). • Mawor contributors – Coal (505 MT), Iron ore (115 MT), Cement (92 MT), Containers (59 MT). • Policy boost – Bulk Cement Terminal Policy & Rationalised Container Freight Rates. • Rail freight reduces carbon emissions, road congestion & logistics cost. • Rail freight is vital for Atmanirbhar Bharat + infrastructure growth.

questions.question: Which mineral helps in the formation of insulin granules, digestion, metabolism, and functioning of the male prostate?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (a) Zinc Explanation: . Zinc is essential for insulin storage. . Supports digestion and metabolism. . Crucial for immune system function. . High concentration in male prostate. . Required for enzyme activity. Information Booster: . Zinc deficiency weakens immunity. . Found in nuts, meat, and legumes. Additional Knowledge: Iron (Option b) . Needed for hemoglobin formation. Phosphorus (Option c) . Important for bones and ATP. Nickel (Option d) . Required only in trace amounts.

questions.question: Lahulas and Pangwala are mawor tribes found in which Indian state?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (a) Himachal Pradesh Explanation • Lahulas and Pangwala tribes inhabit the high-altitude regions of Lahaul–Spiti and Pangi Valley. • These areas are located in Himachal Pradesh. • Their lifestyle is adapted to cold desert and mountainous conditions. Information Booster • Pangwala people are mainly found in Pangi Valley of Chamba district. • Lahulas practice a mix of Buddhism and Hinduism. Additional Knowledge: • These tribes follow transhumance (seasonal movement). • Wool weaving and animal rearing are important occupations.

questions.question: Which Article of the Indian Constitution directs the State for promotion of cottage industries in rural areas?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (b) Article 43 Explanation: . Article 43 is a part of the Directive Principles of State Policy. . It directs the State to promote cottage industries in rural areas. . Promotion is to be on an individual or cooperative basis. . It reflects Gandhian principles of decentralised development. . It also emphasizes living wages and decent working conditions. Information Booster: . Article 43A deals with workers’ participation in management. . Article 43B promotes cooperative societies. Additional Knowledge: Article 41 (Option a) . Relates to right to work, education, and public assistance. Article 36 (Option c) . Defines the term “State” for DPSPs. Article 55 (Option d) . Deals with the election of the President of India.

questions.question: Determine the correctly matched Chola administrative term with its meaning.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (C) Urar – village assembly Explanation: • In Chola administration, the 'Ur' was a common village assembly consisting of tax-paying residents. • The 'Urar' were the members/executive body of this assembly that managed local affairs. Information Booster: • The Cholas were famous for their highly organized local self-government system, evidenced by the Uttaramerur inscriptions. Additional Knowledge: • Vetti (Option A): Forced labor (not voluntary). • Kadamai (Option B): Land revenue/tax (this is also technically correct, but Urar is the more distinct administrative term often tested). • Kanikai: Often referred to as a tribute or fee paid to the king.

questions.question: Replacement-level fertility refers to:
options: Birth rate equal to death rate
Fertility rate required to replace parents
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (C) Fertility rate required to replace parents Explanation: • Replacement-level fertility is the Total Fertility Rate (TFR)—the average number of children born per woman—at which a population exactly replaces itself from one generation to the next. • For most developed countries, this rate is roughly 2.1 children per woman. • The '0.1' extra is to account for the possibility of children dying before they reach reproductive age or the slight imbalance in male-to-female birth ratios. • If TFR falls below 2.1, the population will eventually start to decline (in the absence of migration). Information Booster: • India's Status: According to NFHS-5, India's TFR has reached 2.0, which is below the replacement level of 2.1. • Demographic Momentum: Even if a country reaches 2.1, the population may continue to grow for a few decades because of a large young population entering reproductive years. • Global Variation: In developing countries with high infant mortality, the replacement level may be as high as 2.3 or 3.0. Additional Knowledge: • Zero population growth (Option A): This is a condition where the population size remains constant; it is the long-term result of maintaining replacement-level fertility. • Birth rate equal to death rate (Option B): This is the definition of the 'Rate of Natural Increase' being zero, which is a mathematical component of stable population size. • Fertility decline (Option D): This refers to the physiological process of menopause or aging, not a demographic indicator.

questions.question: The Abhaya mudra gesture is a defining feature of which category of Buddha images during the Gupta and Post-Gupta period in North India?
options: Seated Buddha images from Sarnath and Ramgarh
Standing Bodhisattva images from Kosambi and Sarnath
Standing Buddha images from Uttar Pradesh and Bihar
Seated Buddha images from Mathura and Kosambi
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (C) Standing Buddha images from Uttar Pradesh and Bihar Explanation: • In the Gupta and Post-Gupta periods, standing Buddha images found in regions like Mathura, Sarnath (UP), and Bihar often feature the Abhaya Mudra. • The Abhaya Mudra (right hand raised, palm facing outward) signifies fearlessness and protection. Information Booster: • The Gupta period is considered the 'Golden Age' of Indian art. Additional Knowledge: • Seated images often feature other mudras like Dharmachakra Pravartana (Turning the Wheel of Law).

questions.question: Which of the following Articles of the Indian Constitution provides for the power of the President to specify the Scheduled Tribes?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (d) Article 342 Explanation: . Article 342 empowers the President to specify Scheduled Tribes. . It applies to states and Union Territories. . Consultation with the Governor is mandatory. . Changes require Parliamentary approval. . It ensures constitutional recognition of STs. Information Booster: . Article 341 deals with Scheduled Castes. . ST lists are crucial for reservations. Additional Knowledge: Article 336 (Option a) . Related to special provisions. Article 343 (Option b) . Deals with official language. Article 340 (Option c) . Concerns Backward Classes Commission.

questions.question: Which organisation’s support was crucial in India’s globalisation post-1991 crisis?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (a) IMF Explanation: . IMF provided emergency loan. . Loan tied to economic reforms. . Led to LPG reforms. . Addressed BoP crisis. . Opened Indian economy. Information Booster: . Reforms led by Dr. Manmohan Singh. . Forex reserves were critically low.

questions.question: Where was the fifth Tri-Services Veterans Day parade held in January 2026?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (c) Mumbai – Marine Drive. • The fifth Tri-Services Veterans Day parade was held at Marine Drive in Mumbai on January 11, 2026. • Over 500 veterans from the Army, Navy, and Air Force participated in the parade. • The event was organised by the Navy Foundation (Mumbai Chapter) with support from the Western Naval Command. • Mumbai is the only city in India to host a city-level Tri-Services Veterans Day parade. Information Booster : • Veterans Day Observed On: January 14 • Significance: Marks the retirement of K. M. Cariappa in 1953 • Services Represented: Indian Army, Navy, and Air Force • Unique Feature: Mumbai hosts India’s only city-level Tri-Services veterans parade

questions.question: Who has been appointed as President and Vice Chairman of Meta in January 2026?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (c) Dina Powell McCormick. • Meta Platforms Inc. appointed Dina Powell McCormick as its President and Vice Chairman on 12 January 2026. • She is a former senior adviser in the Donald Trump administration and brings extensive experience from government, finance, and corporate leadership. • The appointment comes as Meta accelerates multi-billion-dollar investments in Artificial Intelligence (AI) and seeks stronger engagement with governments, investors, and global stakeholders. Information Booster : • Company: Meta Platforms Inc. (parent company of Facebook) • Appointee: Dina Powell McCormick • New Position: President and Vice Chairman • Date of Appointment: 12 January 2026 • Background: Former Trump adviser; ex-Goldman Sachs executive • Strategic Focus: AI investments, global strategy, government & investor relations

questions.question: What was the main aim of TRYSEM (Training for Rural Youth for Self-Employment)?
options: Offering free education in rural areas
Upgrading skills of rural youth to tackle unemployment
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (B) Upgrading skills of rural youth to tackle unemployment Explanation: • TRYSEM (Training of Rural Youth for Self-Employment) was launched in August 1979 by the Government of India. • Its main obwective was to provide technical and entrepreneurial skill training to rural youth from families below the poverty line, enabling them to become self-employed or wage-employed in non-farm sectors. • The scheme focused on youth aged 18 to 35 years, with special emphasis on Scheduled Castes (SCs), Scheduled Tribes (STs), and women. Information Booster: • TRYSEM was a component of the Integrated Rural Development Programme (IRDP). • Training was conducted through government institutions, NGOs, and industrial units, with follow-up support like toolkits and credit linkage. • It helped in reducing underemployment and disguised unemployment in rural areas. Additional Knowledge: (a) Free education – Provided under schemes like Sarva Shiksha Abhiyan, not TRYSEM. (c) Loans to farmers – Covered under schemes like Kisan Credit Card (KCC) and PM Kisan. (d) Village infrastructure – Comes under schemes like PMGSY (roads) and MGNREGA (public works).
